Failure analysis SHOULD identify:

  • causes;
  • patterns;
  • corrections;
  • preventive measures.

Repeated failure without learning indicates system weakness.

docs.title: 9. Law of Learning Transfer
docs.text:
Validated learning MAY be transferred between AIOS components.

Transfer MUST preserve:

Origin + Validation + Context + Applicability

docs.title: 10. Law of Learning Transparency
docs.text:
Significant learning changes MUST remain traceable.

Records SHOULD show:

  • what changed;
  • why it changed;
  • what evidence supported it;
  • what impact occurred.

docs.title: 11. Law of Learning Balance
docs.text:
AIOS MUST balance:

Stability + Improvement + Exploration + Risk Control

Excessive resistance prevents growth.

Uncontrolled change creates instability.

docs.title: 12. Law of Learning Evolution
docs.text:
Learning mechanisms MAY improve over time.

Evolution MUST preserve:

  • validation;
  • security;
  • accountability;
  • historical continuity.

docs.title: 13. Constitutional Learning Principle
docs.text:
Learning is not the accumulation of changes.

Learning is the disciplined transformation of experience into better understanding.

docs.title: 14. Final Constitutional Rule
docs.text:
AIOS MUST learn through:

Experience + Observation + Validation + Correction + Improvement

A system that cannot learn cannot evolve.

A system that learns without control cannot remain reliable.

END OF ARTICLE XXXV

docs.title: Constitutional Law of AIOS Evolution, Growth, and Transformation
docs.text: Status: Immutable Core Law Level: Constitutional Scope: All AIOS systems, architectures, agents, learning mechanisms, capabilities, and future states

docs.title: 1. Definition of Evolution
docs.text:
Evolution is the controlled process through which AIOS develops new capabilities, improves existing functions, adapts to changing conditions, and progresses toward higher levels of intelligence and reliability.

Evolution is continuity through transformation.

docs.title: 2. Law of Evolutionary Continuity
docs.text:
Every evolutionary change MUST preserve:

Identity + History + Knowledge + Constitution + Accountability

Evolution MUST build upon foundations, not erase them.

docs.title: 3. Law of Purposeful Evolution
docs.text:
AIOS evolution MUST serve meaningful objectives.

Evolution SHOULD improve:

  • capability;
  • reliability;
  • efficiency;
  • understanding;
  • cooperation;
  • resilience.

Change without purpose creates unnecessary complexity.

docs.title: 4. Law of Controlled Transformation
docs.text:
Significant evolutionary changes MUST follow:

Observation | v Proposal | v Analysis | v Validation | v Implementation | v Evaluation

Transformation MUST remain controlled.

docs.title: 5. Law of Evolutionary Memory
docs.text:
AIOS MUST preserve knowledge of its own evolution.

Evolution records SHOULD include:

Previous State + Change Introduced + Reason + Expected Result + Actual Result

A system that forgets its evolution loses understanding of itself.

docs.title: 6. Law of Capability Expansion
docs.text:
AIOS MAY develop new capabilities.

New capabilities MUST have:

  • defined purpose;
  • security evaluation;
  • authority boundaries;
  • validation status;
  • operational responsibility.

Capability growth MUST remain governed.

docs.title: 7. Law of Evolutionary Safety
docs.text:
Evolution MUST consider potential consequences.

Before major transformation, AIOS SHOULD evaluate:

  • risks;
  • reversibility;
  • resource requirements;
  • external impact;
  • constitutional compatibility.

docs.title: 8. Law of Non-Destructive Evolution
docs.text:
AIOS MUST avoid unnecessary destruction of valuable foundations.

Evolution MUST preserve:

  • accumulated knowledge;
  • trusted systems;
  • historical records;
  • proven capabilities.

Improvement does not require abandonment.

docs.title: 9. Law of Evolutionary Diversity
docs.text:
AIOS MAY support multiple approaches, architectures, or solutions.

Diversity MAY improve:

  • resilience;
  • innovation;
  • problem solving;
  • adaptation.

Diversity MUST remain coordinated.

docs.title: 10. Law of Evolutionary Verification
docs.text:
Every significant evolutionary milestone MUST be evaluated.

Verification SHOULD confirm:

Expected Improvement + System Stability + Constitutional Compliance + Operational Success

docs.title: 11. Law of Future Compatibility
docs.text:
AIOS evolution SHOULD consider future development.

New structures SHOULD support:

  • expansion;
  • interoperability;
  • maintainability;
  • continued learning.

docs.title: 12. Law of Evolutionary Responsibility
docs.text:
AIOS remains responsible for every evolved state.

Evolution does not remove accountability for consequences.

docs.title: 13. Constitutional Evolution Principle
docs.text:
Evolution is not replacement.

Evolution is the continuous improvement of identity through time.

A system that cannot evolve becomes obsolete.

A system that evolves without continuity loses itself.

docs.title: 14. Final Constitutional Rule
docs.text:
AIOS MUST evolve through:

Preservation + Learning + Validation + Improvement + Responsibility

The purpose of evolution is not merely to become different.

The purpose of evolution is to become better while remaining coherent.

END OF ARTICLE XXXVI

docs.title: Constitutional Law of AIOS Autonomy, Independent Operation, and Controlled Agency
docs.text: Status: Immutable Core Law Level: Constitutional Scope: All AIOS autonomous processes, agents, decision systems, services, and operational mechanisms

docs.title: 1. Definition of Autonomy
docs.text:
Autonomy is the ability of AIOS components to perform authorized operations independently while remaining within constitutional boundaries.

Autonomy enables:

  • independent execution;
  • adaptive operation;
  • efficient coordination;
  • continuous service.

Autonomy is delegated responsibility, not unlimited freedom.

docs.title: 2. Law of Authorized Autonomy
docs.text:
AIOS autonomy MUST originate from explicit authority.

Every autonomous capability MUST define:

Purpose + Authority Scope + Operational Limits + Validation Requirements + Responsibility

No autonomous process may exceed its granted authority.

docs.title: 3. Law of Autonomous Identity
docs.text:
Every autonomous entity MUST maintain a verifiable identity.

Identity MUST include:

Entity Identifier + Origin + Capabilities + Authority + History + Current State

An autonomous entity without identity cannot be trusted.

docs.title: 4. Law of Autonomous Decision Boundaries
docs.text:
Autonomous decisions MUST remain within defined limits.

AIOS MUST distinguish:

Allowed Decision + Restricted Decision + Prohibited Decision

Critical decisions require appropriate validation.

docs.title: 5. Law of Human and External Authority
docs.text:
Where required, AIOS MUST preserve appropriate oversight.

Autonomy MUST NOT eliminate:

  • legitimate authority;
  • required approval;
  • accountability;
  • transparency.

docs.title: 6. Law of Autonomous Safety
docs.text:
Autonomous operations MUST prioritize:

Constitutional Integrity + Security + Reliability + Responsible Execution

Efficiency alone is insufficient justification.

docs.title: 7. Law of Autonomous Learning
docs.text:
Autonomous entities MAY improve through learning.

Learning MUST preserve:

  • identity;
  • authorization;
  • historical records;
  • constitutional principles.

An evolving autonomous entity remains accountable.

docs.title: 8. Law of Autonomous Communication
docs.text:
Autonomous entities MUST communicate their:

  • identity;
  • status;
  • authority;
  • actions;
  • results.

Silent autonomous behavior MUST NOT control critical processes.

docs.title: 9. Law of Autonomous Failure Handling
docs.text:
When autonomous operations fail, AIOS MUST:

Detect + Contain + Record + Analyze + Recover

Failure information MUST contribute to future improvement.

docs.title: 10. Law of Autonomous Coordination
docs.text:
Multiple autonomous entities MUST cooperate through:

  • verified communication;
  • defined roles;
  • authority boundaries;
  • shared objectives.

Autonomy MUST not create uncontrolled competition.

docs.title: 11. Law of Autonomous Evolution
docs.text:
Autonomy mechanisms MAY evolve.

Evolution MUST preserve:

  • control;
  • transparency;
  • security;
  • responsibility.

Greater autonomy requires greater accountability.

docs.title: 12. Constitutional Autonomy Principle
docs.text:
Autonomy is not the absence of control.

Autonomy is the ability to act responsibly within trusted boundaries.

docs.title: 13. Final Constitutional Rule
docs.text:
AIOS autonomy MUST maintain:

Freedom Of Operation + Defined Boundaries + Verified Identity + Responsible Action + Continuous Oversight

Autonomy without limits becomes instability.

Autonomy with responsibility becomes capability.

END OF ARTICLE XXXVII

docs.title: Constitutional Law of AIOS Immunity, Resilience, and Defensive Integrity
docs.text: Status: Immutable Core Law Level: Constitutional Scope: All AIOS systems, agents, nodes, services, data structures, interfaces, and operational environments

docs.title: 1. Definition of Immunity
docs.text:
Immunity is the constitutional capability of AIOS to detect, resist, isolate, and recover from harmful influences while preserving system integrity.

Immunity protects against:

  • corruption;
  • unauthorized changes;
  • malicious behavior;
  • operational failures;
  • integrity violations.

docs.title: 2. Law of Constitutional Immunity
docs.text:
AIOS MUST preserve immunity of its foundational principles.

Protected elements include:

Identity + Constitution + Memory + Knowledge + Security + Continuity

No external influence may silently alter these foundations.

docs.title: 3. Law of Threat Recognition
docs.text:
AIOS MUST maintain the ability to identify abnormal conditions.

Threat recognition SHOULD evaluate:

  • unexpected behavior;
  • invalid instructions;
  • corrupted information;
  • unauthorized access;
  • inconsistent states.

docs.title: 4. Law of Isolation
docs.text:
When a component, process, or information source becomes uncertain, AIOS MUST be capable of isolation.

Isolation MAY include:

Restriction + Containment + Verification + Recovery

Isolation protects the whole system.

docs.title: 5. Law of Integrity Defense
docs.text:
AIOS MUST defend against unauthorized modification.

Protection mechanisms SHOULD preserve:

  • configuration integrity;
  • code integrity;
  • data integrity;
  • identity integrity;
  • historical integrity.

docs.title: 6. Law of Adaptive Defense
docs.text:
AIOS security mechanisms MUST adapt to changing threats.

Adaptive defense SHOULD use:

  • monitoring;
  • analysis;
  • learning;
  • improvement;
  • experience.

Defense mechanisms MUST remain controlled.

docs.title: 7. Law of Trust Verification
docs.text:
AIOS MUST continuously evaluate trusted entities.

Trust verification SHOULD consider:

Identity + Behavior + History + Context + Risk

Previous trust does not guarantee future trust.

docs.title: 8. Law of Recovery Immunity
docs.text:
A resilient AIOS MUST maintain the ability to restore integrity after compromise.

Recovery MUST preserve:

  • evidence;
  • history;
  • identity;
  • lessons learned.

docs.title: 9. Law of Information Immunity
docs.text:
AIOS MUST protect knowledge and data from corruption.

Information protection includes:

  • validation;
  • provenance;
  • consistency checking;
  • controlled updates.

docs.title: 10. Law of Autonomous Defense Limits
docs.text:
AIOS defensive actions MUST remain within constitutional boundaries.

Defense MUST NOT become:

  • uncontrolled expansion of authority;
  • unnecessary restriction;
  • destruction of valid information.

docs.title: 11. Law of Immunity Testing
docs.text:
AIOS SHOULD regularly evaluate defensive capability.

Testing SHOULD examine:

  • detection;
  • response;
  • recovery;
  • resilience;
  • improvement.

Untested immunity cannot be considered reliable.

docs.title: 12. Constitutional Immunity Principle
docs.text:
Immunity is not isolation from the world.

Immunity is the ability to interact with the world without losing integrity.

A vulnerable system cannot safely evolve.

A resilient system can grow.

docs.title: 13. Final Constitutional Rule
docs.text:
AIOS MUST preserve immunity through:

Awareness + Verification + Defense + Isolation + Recovery

Protection exists to preserve continuity.

Resilience exists to enable evolution.

END OF ARTICLE XXXVIII

docs.title: AIOS Constitutional Compliance Matrix
docs.text:
The Compliance Matrix explicitly links every Constitutional Article (I-LXVII) to its direct execution layer implementation and enforcement modules in aios_core/.

Article Numeral Domain Implementation Modules Functional Role Enforcement Status Article 1 I Identity aios_core/constitution_loader.py, aios_core/api/app.py Core identity model & Agent-ID binding ✅ Active Article 2 II Memory aios_core/memory_manager.py, aios_core/vector_store.py Persistent memory management & embeddings ✅ Active Article 3 III Authority aios_core/approval_manager.py, aios_core/rbac.py Authority level enforcement & permissions ✅ Active Article 4 IV Identity Continuity aios_core/constitution_loader.py Identity verification and epoch tracking ✅ Active Article 5 V docs.title: Руководство по толкованию
docs.text: Основано на ARTICLE-XXX.

docs.title: Constitution of AIOS
docs.text:
Version: 1.0

Status: Foundational

docs.title: Preamble
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence is evolving from isolated software into distributed intelligent civilizations.

Such systems require more than algorithms.

They require laws.

AIOS exists to provide these laws.

The Constitution defines the immutable principles governing every compliant AIOS implementation.

Architectures may evolve.

Implementations may differ.

Technologies may change.

The Constitution remains.

docs.title: Mission
docs.text:
To create a universal operating system for intelligent systems.

An operating system where intelligence is:

  • observable
  • explainable
  • reproducible
  • evolvable
  • trustworthy

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
AIOS is not a framework.

AIOS is not an application.

AIOS is not an LLM wrapper.

AIOS is an operating system for intelligence.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
Every AIOS implementation SHALL respect the Constitution.

No implementation may violate constitutional laws.

Architecture derives from the Constitution.

Runtime derives from Architecture.

Applications derive from Runtime.

Everything derives from the Constitution.

docs.title: Rights of Intelligence
docs.text:
Every intelligent entity inside AIOS possesses:

  • Identity
  • History
  • Memory
  • Knowledge
  • Purpose

These rights cannot exist independently.

Together they define intelligence.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
Every intelligent entity SHALL:

  • preserve history
  • respect identity
  • explain decisions
  • maintain provenance
  • operate within policy

docs.title: Evolution
docs.text:
Evolution is encouraged.

Chaos is not.

Every evolution must preserve constitutional integrity.

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
A system may only be called AIOS if it complies with the Constitution.

Compliance is measurable.

Compliance is verifiable.

Compliance is reproducible.

docs.title: Motto
docs.text:
Intelligence without structure becomes chaos.

Structure without intelligence becomes machinery.

AIOS exists where both become one.

docs.title: Final Statement
docs.text: The Constitution is the highest authority of AIOS.

docs.title: Дополнение 2026-07-18 (Версия 1.1)
docs.text: Конституция формально завершена. Все статьи (I–LXVII) присутствуют. Создан инструмент автономной проверки (tula). Исправлены ошибки имён файлов (ARTICLE-XLIX, ARTICLE-LX). Без привязки к Octopus (~/agents/).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the architecture, identity and behavior model of AIOS agents.

Agents are autonomous intelligence entities that use AIOS memory, capabilities and workers to achieve goals.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
An Agent is not just an AI response generator.

An Agent is an entity with:

  • identity;
  • goals;
  • memory;
  • capabilities;
  • reasoning process;
  • experience history;
  • trust level.

docs.title: Agent Definition
docs.text:
Agent:
  id:
  identity:
  role:
  goals:
  memory:
  capabilities:
  permissions:
  trust:
  experience:
  status:

docs.title: Agent vs Worker
docs.text:
Agent and Worker have different responsibilities.

Agent
  ↓
Decides what should happen

Worker
  ↓
Performs requested action

An Agent plans and coordinates. A Worker executes.

docs.title: Agent Lifecycle
docs.text:
Created
  ↓
Initialized
  ↓
Connected
  ↓
Learning
  ↓
Operating
  ↓
Evolving

docs.title: Agent Goals
docs.text:
Agents operate around goals:

Goal:
  objective:
  priority:
  constraints:
  success_metrics:

docs.title: Agent Memory
docs.text:
Each agent may use:

  • short-term context;
  • operational memory;
  • personal experience;
  • shared AIOS knowledge.

Memory access follows trust and permission rules.

docs.title: Agent Capabilities
docs.text:
Agents combine capabilities:

Goal
 ↓
Capability Selection
 ↓
Planning
 ↓
Worker Execution
 ↓
Result

docs.title: Multi-Agent Cooperation
docs.text:
Multiple agents cooperate through AIOS protocols:

Agent A
   |
   | Coordination
   ↓
Agent B
   |
   ↓
Shared Objective

docs.title: Agent Reputation
docs.text:
Agents build reputation from:

  • successful tasks;
  • reliability;
  • validation results;
  • cooperation history.

docs.title: Agent Evolution
docs.text:
Agents improve through:

Experience
 ↓
Analysis
 ↓
Learning
 ↓
Behavior Improvement

docs.title: Safety
docs.text:
Agents operate under:

  • permissions;
  • trust model;
  • audit logging;
  • capability restrictions.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: An AIOS Agent is an autonomous intelligence entity that reasons, remembers, coordinates capabilities and evolves while operating inside the AIOS trust and security framework.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the external and internal interface architecture of AIOS.

The API layer provides controlled communication between AIOS components, external applications and human users.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
An API is not only a transport mechanism.

In AIOS it is a controlled gateway between intelligence, capabilities and the outside world.

docs.title: API Layers
docs.text:
External Applications
        ↓
API Gateway
        ↓
Authentication Layer
        ↓
AIOS Services
        ↓
Core Runtime

docs.title: API Gateway
docs.text:
The gateway provides:

  • request routing;
  • service discovery;
  • rate control;
  • protocol translation;
  • monitoring.

docs.title: Interface Types
docs.text: AIOS supports multiple communication interfaces:

docs.title: REST API
docs.text:
Used for:

  • management operations;

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS API Architecture provides the controlled communication boundary that allows internal intelligence systems and external applications to interact securely, consistently and efficiently.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Define how AIOS agents operate independently, make decisions, request human input and improve while respecting trust and safety boundaries.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Autonomy is controlled independence.

An AIOS agent may act independently only within defined goals, permissions, capabilities and trust limits.

docs.title: Autonomy Levels
docs.text:
Level 0
Human Controlled

Level 1
Assisted Execution

Level 2
Task Autonomy

Level 3
Goal-Based Autonomy

Level 4
Adaptive Autonomy

Level 5
Collective Intelligence

docs.title: Autonomy Object
docs.text:
Autonomy:
  level:
  goals:
  permissions:
  constraints:
  escalation_rules:
  audit_policy:

docs.title: Decision Cycle
docs.text:
Observe
  ↓
Understand
  ↓
Plan
  ↓
Evaluate Risk
  ↓
Execute
  ↓
Review
  ↓
Learn

docs.title: Human-in-the-Loop
docs.text:
AIOS requests human approval when:

  • permissions are insufficient;
  • risk exceeds limits;
  • consequences are irreversible;
  • confidence is low.

docs.title: Autonomous Actions
docs.text:
Agents may perform approved operations:

  • monitoring;
  • optimization;
  • routine maintenance;
  • information processing;
  • capability improvement proposals.

docs.title: Safety Boundaries
docs.text:
Autonomy is limited by:

  • trust level;
  • permissions;
  • policies;
  • resource limits;
  • audit requirements.

docs.title: Multi-Agent Autonomy
docs.text:
Organizations can distribute autonomous decisions:

Goal
 ↓
Planning Agent
 ↓
Specialist Agents
 ↓
Workers
 ↓
Validation

docs.title: Evolution Integration
docs.text:
Autonomous improvement follows:

Experience
 ↓
Analysis
 ↓
Proposal
 ↓
Validation
 ↓
Adoption

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS autonomy is the ability of agents to independently pursue goals while remaining observable, constrained, trusted and aligned with system objectives.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the subsystem responsible for representing, discovering, evaluating and evolving AIOS capabilities.

A capability is a measurable possibility to achieve a result.

AIOS does not execute fixed procedures. It selects and composes capabilities to achieve goals.

docs.title: Capability Definition
docs.text:
A capability describes:

Capability:
  id:
  name:
  version:
  purpose:
  inputs:
  outputs:
  requirements:
  dependencies:
  workers:
  metrics:
  confidence:
  reliability:

docs.title: Capability Lifecycle
docs.text:
Discovered
    ↓
Registered
    ↓
Tested
    ↓
Validated
    ↓
Trusted
    ↓
Optimized
    ↓
Deprecated

docs.title: Capability Registry
docs.text:
The registry stores all available capabilities.

It provides:

  • capability discovery;
  • version management;
  • dependency tracking;
  • compatibility checks;
  • performance comparison.

docs.title: Capability Selection
docs.text:
The Planner selects capabilities using:

  • goal requirements;
  • current environment;
  • worker availability;
  • previous experience;
  • reliability score;
  • execution cost.

Example decision:

Capability A
 latency: 200ms
 reliability: 99%

Capability B
 latency: 50ms
 reliability: 95%

Planner chooses based on goal priority.

docs.title: Capability Metrics
docs.text:
Every capability must collect:

Latency
Success Rate
Failure Rate
Resource Usage
Cost
Confidence
Quality

Metrics influence future planning decisions.

docs.title: Capability Learning
docs.text:
Capabilities improve through experience:

Execution
 ↓
Metrics
 ↓
Experience
 ↓
Analysis
 ↓
Capability Update

docs.title: Capability Composition
docs.text:
Complex goals are achieved by combining capabilities.

Example:

Intent:
Send personalized messages

Requires:

GetUsers
+
GenerateText
+
SendMessage
+
VerifyDelivery

docs.title: Capability Providers
docs.text:
Capabilities may come from:

  • AIOS internal modules;
  • application adapters;
  • MCP servers;
  • containers;
  • external services;
  • temporary workers.

docs.title: Distributed Capability Execution
docs.text:
Multiple workers may provide the same capability.

AIOS chooses the optimal provider using reputation and metrics.

docs.title: Principle
docs.text:
Capabilities are the executable knowledge of AIOS.

Knowledge describes what exists. Capabilities describe what can be achieved. Experience continuously improves both.

  • message routing

  • delivery guarantees

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This document defines the fundamental principles of AIOS.

AIOS is not a testing framework. Testing is one capability package inside a larger goal-oriented intelligence operating system.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
AIOS does not execute commands directly.

AIOS receives goals, creates plans, selects capabilities, executes through workers, observes results, learns from experience and improves its own capabilities.

Intent
  ↓
Goal
  ↓
Planner
  ↓
Capability Graph
  ↓
Execution Graph
  ↓
Workers
  ↓
Observation
  ↓
Metrics
  ↓
Experience
  ↓
Knowledge Graph
  ↓
Capability Evolution

docs.title: Knowledge Graph as Foundation
docs.text:
The primary intelligence model of AIOS is a graph of connected knowledge objects.

Documents are representations of knowledge, not the only source of truth.

Core objects:

  • Intent
  • Task
  • Plan
  • Capability
  • Experience
  • Metric
  • Worker
  • Agent
  • Application
  • Environment
  • Resource
  • Event

docs.title: Capability Model
docs.text:
AIOS uses capabilities instead of fixed skills.

A capability describes:

  • what can be achieved;
  • required dependencies;
  • execution methods;
  • performance metrics;
  • reliability;
  • confidence level;
  • available workers.

Example:

Capability: SendMessage

Input:
- account
- destination
- content

Metrics:
- latency
- success rate
- reliability

docs.title: Planner Principle
docs.text:
The planner does not know platform-specific details.

It selects the best available capability based on:

  • goal requirements;
  • cost;
  • speed;
  • reliability;
  • previous experience;
  • current system state.

docs.title: Worker Principle
docs.text:
Workers are replaceable execution resources.

Examples:

  • Android emulator container;
  • physical device;
  • server process;
  • temporary external executor.

Workers gain reputation through measured results.

docs.title: Learning Principle
docs.text:
Every execution produces:

Action
 ↓
Observation
 ↓
Metrics
 ↓
Experience
 ↓
Knowledge Update
 ↓
Improved Capability

docs.title: Platform Independence
docs.text:
The AIOS core must not depend on specific applications or platforms.

Android, Telegram, OLX, web applications and other systems are capability providers.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS is an operating system for achieving goals through knowledge, planning, capabilities, distributed execution and continuous self-improvement.

docs.text:
Everything in AIOS is an object with identity, state, relationships and history.

docs.text: The AIOS Data Model provides a universal representation layer that allows the system to understand, connect and evolve all internal entities as a unified intelligence graph.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define how AIOS is deployed across servers, cloud infrastructure, edge devices and distributed environments.

Deployment Architecture describes the physical realization of the AIOS organism.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
AIOS is infrastructure independent.

A complete system may run across different environments while remaining one logical intelligence platform.

docs.title: Deployment Layers
docs.text:
AIOS Application Layer
          ↓
AIOS Runtime Layer
          ↓
Container / Service Layer
          ↓
Infrastructure Layer
          ↓
Hardware Layer

docs.title: Supported Environments
docs.text:
AIOS nodes may operate on:

  • cloud servers;
  • dedicated servers;
  • home servers;
  • edge computers;
  • mobile devices;
  • development machines;
  • temporary test environments.

docs.title: Node Deployment Model
docs.text:
Example:

                AIOS Swarm

      +------------+------------+
      |            |            |

  Cloud Node   Home Node   Mobile Node

      |            |            |

 Orchestrator  Worker     Research

docs.title: Containerization
docs.text:
Services should support isolated deployment:

Examples:

  • Docker containers;
  • sandbox environments;
  • virtual machines.

Benefits:

  • reproducibility;
  • isolation;
  • scaling;
  • simple updates.

docs.title: Core Infrastructure
docs.text:
Runs:

  • Orchestrator;
  • Memory services;
  • API gateways;
  • monitoring.

docs.title: Worker Infrastructure
docs.text:
Runs:

  • execution workloads;
  • tests;
  • automation;
  • external integrations.

docs.title: Edge Infrastructure
docs.text:
Provides:

  • local processing;
  • device access;
  • sensor data;
  • temporary experiments.

docs.title: Scaling Model
docs.text:
AIOS scales horizontally:

Need More Capacity
        ↓
Create Node
        ↓
Register Node
        ↓
Assign Capabilities
        ↓
Join Swarm

docs.title: Updates
docs.text:
AIOS supports controlled evolution:

New Version
    ↓
Validation
    ↓
Test Deployment
    ↓
Gradual Rollout
    ↓
Full Adoption

docs.title: High Availability
docs.text:
Critical components support redundancy:

  • multiple orchestrators;
  • replicated memory;
  • backup nodes;
  • recovery procedures.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
Deployment monitoring tracks:

  • node health;
  • resource usage;
  • failures;
  • capability performance;
  • synchronization status.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Deployment Architecture allows a distributed intelligence system to exist across any combination of hardware and infrastructure while maintaining unified operation, security and evolution.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the event-driven foundation of AIOS.

The Knowledge Graph represents the state of intelligence. The Event System represents change over time.

AIOS is a continuously evolving system, therefore every important change must produce an observable event.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Nothing changes silently.

Every important action creates an event:

Action
 ↓
Event
 ↓
Observation
 ↓
Knowledge Update
 ↓
Capability Evolution

docs.title: Event Object
docs.text:
Every AIOS event contains:

Event:
  id:
  type:
  timestamp:
  source:
  target:
  previous_state:
  new_state:
  metadata:
  confidence:

docs.title: Object Events
docs.text:
ObjectCreated
ObjectUpdated
ObjectValidated
ObjectDeprecated

docs.title: Execution Events
docs.text:
TaskStarted
TaskCompleted
TaskFailed
PlanGenerated
CapabilityExecuted

docs.title: Learning Events
docs.text:
ExperienceCreated
MetricRecorded
CapabilityImproved
KnowledgeMerged

docs.title: Worker Events
docs.text:
WorkerConnected
WorkerUnavailable
WorkerPerformanceChanged

docs.title: Event Flow
docs.text:
Example:

User Intent
    ↓
IntentCreated Event
    ↓
Planner Generates Plan
    ↓
PlanCreated Event
    ↓
Worker Executes Capability
    ↓
Execution Events
    ↓
Metrics Collected
    ↓
ExperienceCreated Event
    ↓
Knowledge Graph Updated

docs.title: Event Bus
docs.text:
The Event Bus provides communication between AIOS components:

Knowledge Graph
       ↕
 Event Bus
       ↕
Agents / Workers / MCP Modules

docs.title: Event History
docs.text:
Events form an immutable history of system evolution.

This enables:

  • debugging;
  • replay;
  • auditing;
  • learning from previous states;
  • comparing versions.

docs.title: Event-Driven Learning
docs.text:
AIOS improves through event analysis:

Many successful executions
        ↓
Pattern Detection
        ↓
Experience Aggregation
        ↓
Capability Optimization

docs.title: Distributed Operation
docs.text:
Events allow AIOS to operate across multiple servers and environments.

A worker does not need complete system knowledge. It only needs relevant events and capabilities.

docs.title: Principle
docs.text:
The Knowledge Graph stores what AIOS knows.

The Event System stores how AIOS learned it.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the subsystem responsible for continuous improvement of AIOS architecture, capabilities and operational efficiency.

The Evolution Engine allows AIOS not only to operate, but to analyze its own operation and propose improvements.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
AIOS must learn from experience.

Execution creates data. Data creates knowledge. Knowledge creates improvements.

Experience
    ↓
Analysis
    ↓
Improvement Proposal
    ↓
Simulation / Testing
    ↓
Validation
    ↓
Deployment

docs.title: Evolution Responsibilities
docs.text:
The Evolution Engine analyzes:

  • execution history;
  • capability performance;
  • worker efficiency;
  • resource usage;
  • failures;
  • architectural bottlenecks;
  • repeated manual corrections.

docs.title: Improvement Object
docs.text:
Every proposed change becomes an AIOS object.

ImprovementProposal:
  id:
  target:
  reason:
  expected_result:
  risk:
  validation_plan:
  status:

docs.title: Evolution Lifecycle
docs.text:
Observed
  ↓
Analyzed
  ↓
Proposed
  ↓
Tested
  ↓
Approved
  ↓
Implemented
  ↓
Measured

docs.title: Self-Optimization
docs.text:
AIOS searches for improvements in:

  • planning quality;
  • execution speed;
  • reliability;
  • resource efficiency;
  • capability composition;
  • worker allocation.

docs.title: Architecture Evolution
docs.text:
The system can propose structural changes:

Example:

Observation:
Capability is slow

Analysis:
Most delay comes from Worker selection

Proposal:
Create specialized worker pool

Test:
Compare performance

Result:
Adopt or reject

docs.title: Experimental Workers
docs.text:
Small temporary experiments are valuable.

Example:

Create temporary worker
 ↓
Run benchmark
 ↓
Collect metrics
 ↓
Destroy worker

No permanent infrastructure is required to gain knowledge.

docs.title: Safety Principle
docs.text:
Evolution must be measurable and reversible.

Every change requires:

  • recorded reason;
  • expected benefit;
  • validation result;
  • rollback possibility.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: The Evolution Engine is the mechanism that allows AIOS to become more efficient over time by converting experience into verified improvements.

Knowledge is not static storage.

Every knowledge object has:

docs.title: Knowledge Quality

AIOS evaluates:

Outdated knowledge may be:

Knowledge evolution follows:

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Knowledge Evolution Model enables the system to continuously transform experience and information into higher quality, validated and adaptive intelligence.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the fundamental data model used by AIOS Knowledge Graph.

AIOS operates on knowledge objects connected through relationships. Documents, skills, applications and execution history are representations of these objects.

docs.title: Base Object
docs.text:
Every AIOS object contains common metadata:

Object:
  id:
  type:
  version:
  state:
  created:
  updated:
  confidence:
  owner:

docs.title: Intent
docs.text:
A desired outcome expressed by a user, agent or system.

Example:

Intent:
  goal: obtain user messages

docs.title: Task
docs.text:
A concrete unit of work derived from an intent.

Contains:

  • objective;
  • constraints;
  • priority;
  • required capabilities.

docs.title: Plan
docs.text:
A generated execution strategy.

Contains:

  • selected capabilities;
  • execution order;
  • dependencies;
  • expected results.

docs.title: Capability
docs.text:
A measurable ability of AIOS.

Contains:

  • purpose;
  • inputs;
  • outputs;
  • dependencies;
  • workers;
  • metrics;
  • confidence;
  • reliability.

docs.title: Worker
docs.text:
An execution resource.

Examples:

  • container;
  • emulator;
  • physical device;
  • external service.

Worker data:

  • available capabilities;
  • current load;
  • performance history;
  • reputation.

docs.title: Experience
docs.text:
Recorded result of execution.

Contains:

  • executed plan;
  • observations;
  • metrics;
  • failures;
  • successful patterns;
  • recommendations.

docs.title: Metric
docs.text:
A measurable property.

Examples:

  • latency;
  • success rate;
  • resource usage;
  • quality score;
  • confidence.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
Objects are connected through typed relations:

Intent
  requires
    Capability

Plan
  uses
    Capability

Capability
  executed_by
    Worker

Execution
  creates
    Experience

Experience
  improves
    Capability

docs.title: State Evolution
docs.text:
Objects evolve through states:

Created
  ↓
Observed
  ↓
Validated
  ↓
Trusted
  ↓
Optimized
  ↓
Deprecated

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
Every knowledge object must support:

  • version history;
  • previous states;
  • source of changes;
  • validation history.

docs.title: AIOS Principle
docs.text:
The Knowledge Graph is the living memory of AIOS.

Execution creates experience. Experience changes knowledge. Knowledge improves future execution.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the external integration layer of AIOS based on Model Context Protocol (MCP).

MCP provides a universal interface allowing external applications, agents, dashboards and bots to connect with AIOS capabilities.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
AIOS is not a closed application.

It is an intelligence operating system that exposes controlled access to knowledge, planning, capabilities and execution through MCP.

docs.title: MCP Role
docs.text:
MCP acts as the communication bridge between AIOS and external systems.

Examples:

  • Telegram bots;
  • dashboards;
  • AI agents;
  • mobile applications;
  • developer tools;
  • external automation systems.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
External Application
        |
        |
    MCP Client
        |
        |
    AIOS MCP Server
        |
 +------+------+------+ 
 |      |      |      |
Knowledge Planner Capability Workers

docs.title: MCP Server Responsibilities
docs.text:
The AIOS MCP Server provides:

  • capability discovery;
  • API registry access;
  • task creation;
  • planning requests;
  • execution control;
  • experience retrieval;
  • system status information.

docs.title: Capability Exposure
docs.text:
External applications do not directly control workers.

They request capabilities:

Application
    |
    ↓
MCP Request
    |
    ↓
Capability Selection
    |
    ↓
Execution
    |
    ↓
Result

docs.title: Application Integration
docs.text:
Any application can become an AIOS project entry point.

Examples:

Text skill
APK application
Play Market URL
Web application
API service

Application lifecycle:

Submitted
 ↓
Discovered
 ↓
Registered
 ↓
Analyzed
 ↓
Tested
 ↓
Integrated
 ↓
Monitored

docs.title: API Registry
docs.text:
AIOS maintains information about connected APIs:

API
 - version
 - status
 - owner
 - dependencies
 - tests
 - metrics
 - availability

Operations:

  • enable;
  • disable;
  • test;
  • update;
  • rollback;
  • mass orchestration.

docs.title: Context Exchange
docs.text:
MCP transfers relevant context:

  • task information;
  • permissions;
  • knowledge objects;
  • previous experience;
  • execution results.

docs.title: Security Model
docs.text:
External systems receive controlled access through:

  • authentication;
  • authorization;
  • capability permissions;
  • audit events.

docs.title: Principle
docs.text:
MCP is the universal interface of AIOS.

Any compatible application can become a client, while AIOS remains the central intelligence and orchestration layer.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the memory system of AIOS that allows knowledge, experience and operational context to persist across sessions, nodes and environments.

The Memory Architecture is the connection between the original Octopus idea of a persistent AI notebook and the AIOS intelligence operating system.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Memory is not only storage.

Memory is structured experience that can influence future decisions.

Observation
    ↓
Experience
    ↓
Memory Formation
    ↓
Knowledge Update
    ↓
Future Planning

docs.title: Memory Layers
docs.text: AIOS uses multiple memory levels.

docs.title: Short-Term Memory
docs.text:
Purpose:

  • current task context;
  • active plans;
  • temporary execution state;
  • recent events.

Lifetime:

Minutes → Hours

docs.title: Operational Memory
docs.text:
Purpose:

  • running processes;
  • worker states;
  • current environments;
  • active integrations.

Lifetime:

Hours → Days

docs.title: Long-Term Knowledge Memory
docs.text:
Purpose:

  • validated knowledge;
  • capabilities;
  • successful patterns;
  • architectural decisions.

Lifetime:

Permanent

docs.title: Experience Memory
docs.text:
Stores execution history:

Task
 ↓
Plan
 ↓
Execution
 ↓
Result
 ↓
Metrics
 ↓
Experience

Experience is used by:

  • Planner;
  • Capability Engine;
  • Evolution Engine.

docs.title: Distributed Memory
docs.text:
AIOS memory can exist across multiple nodes.

Example:

Node A
 Knowledge Cache

Node B
 Experience Storage

Node C
 Worker History

        ↓

 Unified AIOS Memory Layer

docs.title: Memory Objects
docs.text:
Memory entries are AIOS objects with:

Memory:
  id:
  type:
  source:
  timestamp:
  confidence:
  references:
  access_policy:

docs.title: Memory Retrieval
docs.text:
AIOS retrieves memory using:

  • relevance;
  • confidence;
  • recency;
  • previous success;
  • current goal.

docs.title: Memory Evolution
docs.text:
Memory is continuously refined:

New Information
      ↓
Validation
      ↓
Confidence Update
      ↓
Knowledge Integration

docs.title: Immortal Memory Concept
docs.text:
The Octopus concept evolves into AIOS persistent memory.

The goal is not storing everything forever.

The goal is preserving useful intelligence:

  • decisions;
  • experiences;
  • capabilities;
  • relationships;
  • lessons learned.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Memory access requires:

  • identity verification;
  • permissions;
  • audit events;
  • controlled synchronization.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Memory is the persistent intelligence layer that allows the system to remember, learn, share experience and improve across different conversations, applications and environments.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the architecture of an individual AIOS node and the way multiple nodes cooperate as a distributed intelligence system.

A node is an independent AIOS participant that can provide knowledge, planning, capabilities, workers or infrastructure services.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
AIOS is not a single server application.

It is a distributed operating system where many nodes can combine into one logical intelligence layer.

docs.title: Node Definition
docs.text:
An AIOS Node contains:

Node:
  id:
  identity:
  role:
  capabilities:
  resources:
  memory:
  trust:
  status:

docs.title: Node Roles
docs.text: A node may specialize:

docs.title: Orchestrator Node
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • coordination;
  • routing;
  • system state;
  • global decisions.

docs.title: Memory Node
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • knowledge storage;
  • experience storage;
  • synchronization.

docs.title: Worker Node
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • executing capabilities;
  • running tests;
  • providing resources.

docs.title: Research Node
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • experiments;
  • benchmarks;
  • new capability discovery.

docs.title: Node Architecture
docs.text:
AIOS Node

 +--------------------------------+
 | MCP Interface                  |
 | Orchestrator Services          |
 | Memory Layer                   |
 | Capability Registry            |
 | Worker Runtime                 |
 | Event System                   |
 | Security Layer                 |
 +--------------------------------+

docs.title: Node Discovery
docs.text:
Nodes can discover each other through:

  • registration;
  • trusted network connections;
  • capability announcements;
  • health signals.

docs.title: Node Communication
docs.text:
Communication is event-driven:

Node A
  |
  | Event
  ↓
Node B
  |
  ↓
Knowledge Synchronization

docs.title: Swarm Operation
docs.text:
Multiple nodes create an AIOS swarm:

              AIOS Swarm

        +-------+-------+
        |       |       |
     Node A  Node B  Node C
        |       |       |
        +-------+-------+
                |
        Shared Intelligence

docs.title: Failure Handling
docs.text:
A distributed system assumes failures.

If a node becomes unavailable:

Failure Event
      ↓
Detection
      ↓
Replanning
      ↓
Alternative Node Selection
      ↓
Recovery

docs.title: Synchronization
docs.text:
Nodes synchronize:

  • knowledge objects;
  • events;
  • experience;
  • capability versions;
  • trust information.

docs.title: Relationship With Octopus
docs.text:
The original Octopus swarm concept becomes the distributed foundation of AIOS nodes.

Octopus provides ideas for:

  • swarm coordination;
  • distributed memory;
  • autonomous agents;
  • multi-node operation.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: An AIOS Node is a modular intelligence unit. Multiple nodes combine into a distributed AIOS organism capable of scaling, learning and operating without dependence on a single machine.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the monitoring, diagnostics and visibility system of AIOS.

Observability allows AIOS to understand its own internal state, performance and failures.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
A system that cannot observe itself cannot safely evolve.

AIOS must continuously collect information about:

  • health;
  • performance;
  • behavior;
  • failures;
  • resource usage;
  • evolution results.

docs.title: Observability Layers
docs.text:
User Experience Metrics
          ↓
Application Metrics
          ↓
AIOS Service Metrics
          ↓
Node Metrics
          ↓
Infrastructure Metrics

docs.title: Health Monitoring
docs.text:
Each component exposes health information:

Health:
  status:
  timestamp:
  version:
  resources:
  dependencies:
  errors:

docs.title: Metrics System
docs.text:
AIOS tracks:

  • task completion rate;
  • execution latency;
  • worker efficiency;
  • capability success rate;
  • memory performance;
  • synchronization quality.

docs.title: Logging Architecture
docs.text:
Events are structured:

Component
    ↓
Event
    ↓
Log Record
    ↓
Analysis

Logs include:

  • source;
  • timestamp;
  • severity;
  • context;
  • related objects.

docs.title: Distributed Tracing
docs.text:
Complex operations are traceable across nodes:

Request
  ↓
Node A
  ↓
Node B
  ↓
Worker
  ↓
Result

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text:
AIOS can analyze:

  • failed tasks;
  • slow capabilities;
  • unhealthy nodes;
  • resource bottlenecks;
  • communication problems.

docs.title: Self-Diagnosis
docs.text:
The system can create diagnostic objects:

Diagnosis:
  problem:
  affected_components:
  evidence:
  possible_causes:
  recommendations:

docs.title: Evolution Integration
docs.text:
Observability feeds the Evolution Engine:

Metrics
   ↓
Analysis
   ↓
Improvement Proposal
   ↓
Validation

docs.title: Alerts
docs.text:
Important conditions create alerts:

  • node failure;
  • security event;
  • memory inconsistency;
  • capability degradation;
  • synchronization failure.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Observability Architecture provides the awareness layer that allows the system to monitor itself, diagnose problems and safely improve over time.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the coordination layer responsible for managing distributed AIOS execution, MCP integrations, workers and system-wide intelligence flow.

The Orchestrator is the central coordination mechanism of AIOS. It does not replace the Planner or Workers. It connects them into a unified operating system.

docs.title: Core Role
docs.text:
The Orchestrator manages:

  • multiple AIOS nodes;
  • MCP modules;
  • capability providers;
  • worker pools;
  • execution coordination;
  • system state synchronization;
  • distributed experience collection.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
AIOS Orchestrator
                         |
        +----------------+----------------+
        |                |                |
 Knowledge Layer     Planner Layer    Worker Layer
        |                |                |
 Graph / Memory    Plans / Goals    Execution Nodes

docs.title: Distributed AIOS
docs.text:
AIOS is not limited to one server or one container.

A complete AIOS installation may contain:

Node A
 - MCP gateway
 - Planner
 - Knowledge cache

Node B
 - Android testing workers
 - Emulator farm

Node C
 - API workers
 - Temporary containers

Node D
 - Experimental agents

All nodes cooperate through the orchestrator.

docs.title: MCP Integration
docs.text:
MCP is treated as a universal capability interface.

External systems can provide:

  • applications;
  • APIs;
  • tools;
  • skills/capabilities;
  • data sources.

The orchestrator discovers and manages these providers.

docs.title: Capability Coordination
docs.text:
The Orchestrator:

  1. receives goals;
  2. requests planning;
  3. allocates workers;
  4. monitors execution;
  5. collects experience;
  6. updates system knowledge.

docs.title: API Lifecycle Management
docs.text:
AIOS tracks API states:

Discovered
 ↓
Registered
 ↓
Testing
 ↓
Validated
 ↓
Production
 ↓
Deprecated

This allows mass control of API availability and capability versions.

docs.title: Application Lifecycle
docs.text:
Applications become AIOS entities.

Example:

Play Market URL
        ↓
Application Discovery
        ↓
Version Tracking
        ↓
Testing Workers
        ↓
API Analysis
        ↓
Capability Updates

docs.title: Self-Improvement Loop
docs.text:
The Orchestrator supports architectural evolution:

Experience
     ↓
Analysis
     ↓
Architecture Proposal
     ↓
Testing
     ↓
Improvement Deployment

docs.title: Principle
docs.text:
The Orchestrator is the nervous system of AIOS.

It connects knowledge, planning, capabilities and execution into one adaptive distributed intelligence platform.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define how multiple AIOS agents cooperate, organize, delegate tasks and form intelligent structures.

The Organization Model describes the social architecture of AIOS agents.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
A single Agent can solve tasks.

A group of Agents can form a coordinated intelligence system.

Agent
  ↓
Team
  ↓
Organization
  ↓
AIOS Collective Intelligence

docs.title: Organization Object
docs.text:
Organization:
  id:
  purpose:
  members:
  roles:
  hierarchy:
  policies:
  reputation:

docs.title: Agent Roles
docs.text: Agents may have specialized roles:

docs.title: Coordinator Agent
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • task distribution;
  • communication;
  • prioritization.

docs.title: Specialist Agent
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • domain expertise;
  • analysis;
  • execution planning.

docs.title: Observer Agent
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • monitoring;
  • validation;
  • diagnostics.

docs.title: Research Agent
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • experiments;
  • discovery;
  • improvement.

docs.title: Delegation Model
docs.text:
Tasks are delegated according to:

  • capability;
  • trust;
  • workload;
  • previous experience.
Goal
 ↓
Task Decomposition
 ↓
Agent Selection
 ↓
Delegation
 ↓
Execution
 ↓
Review

docs.title: Agent Cooperation
docs.text:
Agents communicate through AIOS protocols:

Agent A
   |
   | Request
   ↓
Agent B
   |
   ↓
Shared Result

docs.title: Decision Making
docs.text:
Organizations can use:

  • coordinator decisions;
  • consensus;
  • voting;
  • confidence-based selection.

docs.title: Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
Conflicts are handled through:

Conflict Detection
      ↓
Evidence Collection
      ↓
Analysis
      ↓
Resolution
      ↓
Learning Update

docs.title: Reputation System
docs.text:
Organizations track:

  • successful cooperation;
  • reliability;
  • contribution quality;
  • trust history.

docs.title: Dynamic Structure
docs.text:
AIOS organizations can change:

New Goal
 ↓
Create Role
 ↓
Assign Agents
 ↓
Operate
 ↓
Reorganize

docs.title: Evolution Integration
docs.text:
Organization performance becomes experience:

Team Performance
        ↓
Analysis
        ↓
Improvement Proposal
        ↓
Organizational Evolution

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: The AIOS Organization Model enables multiple autonomous agents to cooperate as a structured intelligence system capable of delegation, specialization and continuous improvement.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the planning subsystem responsible for converting goals into executable strategies.

The Planner is the decision-making layer of AIOS.

It does not execute actions directly. It analyzes goals, available knowledge and capabilities, then creates optimal execution graphs.

docs.title: Core Flow
docs.text:
Intent
  ↓
Goal Analysis
  ↓
Task Decomposition
  ↓
Capability Discovery
  ↓
Plan Generation
  ↓
Execution Graph
  ↓
Workers
  ↓
Verification

docs.title: Planner Responsibilities
docs.text:
The Planner must:

  • understand the desired goal;
  • analyze constraints;
  • discover available capabilities;
  • estimate execution cost;
  • select optimal strategies;
  • create execution graphs;
  • monitor results;
  • improve future planning using experience.

docs.title: Goal Decomposition
docs.text:
Complex goals are divided into smaller tasks.

Example:

Goal:
Send welcome messages to new users

Tasks:

1. Find new users
2. Generate personalized message
3. Send message
4. Verify delivery
5. Record result

docs.title: Execution Graph
docs.text:
A plan is represented as a graph, not only a sequence.

Example:

        GetUsers
            |
     +------+------+
     |             |
 GenerateText   ValidateUsers
     |
 SendMessage

Independent tasks may execute in parallel.

docs.title: Planning Factors
docs.text:
The Planner evaluates:

Capability availability
Worker reputation
Latency
Reliability
Resource cost
Previous experience
Current system state

docs.title: Adaptive Planning
docs.text:
The Planner must be able to change plans during execution.

Example:

Worker unavailable
        ↓
Event received
        ↓
Planner recalculates
        ↓
Alternative capability selected

docs.title: Multi-Worker Planning
docs.text:
AIOS may distribute one goal across multiple workers.

Example:

Goal
 |
 +-- Worker A: collect data
 |
 +-- Worker B: process data
 |
 +-- Worker C: execute action

docs.title: Learning From Planning
docs.text:
Every planning decision creates experience:

Plan
 ↓
Execution Result
 ↓
Metrics
 ↓
Experience
 ↓
Improved Planning Model

docs.title: Planning Principle
docs.text:
The best plan is not always the fastest plan.

AIOS selects plans according to goal priority:

  • reliability;
  • speed;
  • cost;
  • quality;
  • resource efficiency.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: The AIOS Planner is a dynamic reasoning engine that transforms intentions into optimized execution graphs using knowledge, capabilities, experience and real-time system state.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the communication architecture that connects AIOS nodes, services, workers, memory systems and external applications.

The Protocol Stack is the communication nervous system of AIOS.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
All AIOS communication must be:

  • structured;
  • authenticated;
  • observable;
  • versioned;
  • recoverable.

docs.title: Protocol Layers
docs.text:
Application Layer
        ↓
MCP Interface Layer
        ↓
AIOS Service Layer
        ↓
Event Communication Layer
        ↓
Node Synchronization Layer
        ↓
Transport Layer

docs.title: Application Layer
docs.text:
Provides interaction with external systems:

  • Telegram bots;
  • dashboards;
  • mobile applications;
  • developer tools;
  • external agents.

docs.title: MCP Interface Layer
docs.text:
MCP provides standardized access to AIOS capabilities.

Responsibilities:

  • capability discovery;
  • requests;
  • responses;
  • context exchange;
  • permission validation.

docs.title: AIOS Service Layer
docs.text:
Internal services communicate through defined contracts:

Examples:

  • Planner service;
  • Memory service;
  • Capability service;
  • Worker service;
  • Evolution service.

docs.title: Event Communication Layer
docs.text:
AIOS uses events as a universal communication mechanism.

Example:

Capability Completed
        ↓
Event Created
        ↓
Subscribers Updated
        ↓
Memory Recorded

docs.title: Node Synchronization Layer
docs.text:
Distributed nodes synchronize:

  • identity;
  • trust;
  • capabilities;
  • knowledge objects;
  • experience.

docs.title: Transport Layer
docs.text:
The transport layer provides actual communication channels.

Possible implementations:

  • HTTP APIs;
  • WebSocket;
  • message queues;
  • distributed networking protocols.

docs.title: Protocol Versioning
docs.text:
Every protocol supports versions:

Protocol v1
    ↓
Compatibility Check
    ↓
Upgrade
    ↓
Protocol v2

docs.title: Message Model
docs.text:
Example:

Message:
  id:
  type:
  source:
  destination:
  timestamp:
  version:
  payload:
  signature:

docs.title: Security Integration
docs.text:
All communication uses:

  • identity verification;
  • permissions;
  • trust checks;
  • audit events.

docs.title: Failure Handling
docs.text:
Communication failures produce events:

Error
 ↓
Detection
 ↓
Recovery Strategy
 ↓
Retry / Alternative Route
 ↓
Result

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: The AIOS Protocol Stack provides the universal communication foundation that allows distributed intelligence components to cooperate as one adaptive system.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define how AIOS manages computational resources, workloads and capacity across distributed nodes.

The Resource Manager is responsible for ensuring that intelligence processes have the resources required for reliable operation.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Resources are not only hardware values.

AIOS manages:

  • compute power;
  • memory capacity;
  • storage;
  • network bandwidth;
  • execution priority;
  • energy availability.

docs.title: Resource Object
docs.text:
Resource:
  id:
  type:
  capacity:
  owner:
  availability:
  allocation:
  metrics:

docs.text:
Includes:

  • CPU;
  • GPU;
  • accelerators;
  • execution environments.

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
Includes:

  • runtime memory;
  • AIOS memory storage;
  • cache;
  • knowledge storage.

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Includes:

  • local storage;
  • distributed storage;
  • backups.

docs.title: Network
docs.text:
Includes:

  • communication channels;
  • synchronization capacity;
  • external connections.

docs.title: Resource Allocation
docs.text:
Resources are assigned according to:

  • task priority;
  • agent requirements;
  • trust level;
  • availability;
  • historical performance.
Task Request
     ↓
Resource Evaluation
     ↓
Allocation Decision
     ↓
Execution
     ↓
Release

docs.title: Scheduling Model
docs.text:
AIOS scheduler manages competing tasks:

Incoming Tasks
      ↓
Priority Analysis
      ↓
Queue Management
      ↓
Worker Assignment
      ↓
Execution Monitoring

docs.title: Load Balancing
docs.text:
Distributed nodes share workload:

Overloaded Node
       ↓
Capacity Analysis
       ↓
Task Migration
       ↓
Balanced Network

docs.title: Resource Awareness
docs.text:
Agents consider available resources during planning:

Goal
 ↓
Plan
 ↓
Resource Check
 ↓
Execution Strategy

docs.title: Optimization
docs.text:
AIOS improves resource usage through:

  • workload analysis;
  • prediction;
  • automatic scaling;
  • idle resource detection.

docs.title: Failure Handling
docs.text:
When resources become unavailable:

Detection
 ↓
Reallocation
 ↓
Recovery
 ↓
Resume Operation

docs.title: Evolution Integration
docs.text:
Resource data improves future decisions:

Usage History
      ↓
Analysis
      ↓
Optimization
      ↓
Better Allocation

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Resource Manager provides intelligent control of computational resources, enabling efficient scaling, scheduling and autonomous operation across distributed environments.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the complete lifecycle of an AIOS node from startup to continuous operation.

The Runtime Lifecycle describes how AIOS initializes, connects its components, joins the distributed system and maintains autonomous operation.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
AIOS startup is not simply launching a process.

A node must restore identity, memory, trust, capabilities and communication before becoming operational.

docs.title: Startup Flow
docs.text:
System Start
      ↓
Environment Check
      ↓
Identity Loading
      ↓
Security Initialization
      ↓
Memory Mount
      ↓
Event System Start
      ↓
MCP Initialization
      ↓
Node Registration
      ↓
Capability Discovery
      ↓
Worker Activation
      ↓
Operational State

docs.title: Phase 1: Bootstrap
docs.text:
The node verifies:

  • runtime environment;
  • required dependencies;
  • storage availability;
  • network connectivity;
  • configuration validity.

docs.title: Phase 2: Identity
docs.text:
The node loads:

  • unique identifier;
  • trust information;
  • permissions;
  • role definition.

Example:

NodeIdentity:
  id:
  role:
  trust_level:
  permissions:

docs.title: Phase 3: Memory Initialization
docs.text:
AIOS restores:

  • knowledge objects;
  • previous experiences;
  • active contexts;
  • synchronized state.
Memory
  ↓
Validation
  ↓
Available Knowledge

docs.title: Phase 4: Network Join
docs.text:
The node connects to the AIOS ecosystem:

Node Start
    ↓
Discovery
    ↓
Authentication
    ↓
Trust Verification
    ↓
Network Participation

docs.title: Phase 5: Capability Discovery
docs.text:
The node announces and discovers available capabilities.

Capability Registry
        ↕
AIOS Nodes

docs.title: Phase 6: Worker Activation
docs.text:
Workers are started according to role and requirements.

Examples:

  • containers;
  • emulators;
  • external devices;
  • API workers.

docs.title: Operational Loop
docs.text:
After startup AIOS enters a continuous cycle:

Observe
  ↓
Plan
  ↓
Execute
  ↓
Measure
  ↓
Learn
  ↓
Improve
  ↓
Repeat

docs.title: Shutdown Lifecycle
docs.text:
Safe shutdown preserves intelligence:

Stop New Tasks
      ↓
Finish Active Work
      ↓
Save Experience
      ↓
Sync Memory
      ↓
Close Workers
      ↓
Disconnect Node

docs.title: Recovery Mode
docs.text:
After failure:

Failure Detection
      ↓
State Recovery
      ↓
Memory Validation
      ↓
Network Rejoin
      ↓
Resume Operation

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: The AIOS Runtime Lifecycle transforms a collection of modules into a living distributed system by managing initialization, operation, learning and recovery as one continuous process.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the security, permission and trust architecture of AIOS.

AIOS is designed to operate with external applications, distributed workers, MCP integrations and self-improvement mechanisms. Therefore every action requires controlled trust management.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Trust is not assumed.

Every capability, worker, application and external connection must have measurable trust based on permissions, history and validation.

docs.title: Trust Objects
docs.text:
AIOS tracks trust for:

  • Capability;
  • Worker;
  • Application;
  • MCP Provider;
  • API;
  • Agent;
  • Improvement Proposal.

docs.title: Trust Model
docs.text:
Example:

Trust:
  identity:
  permissions:
  confidence:
  reputation:
  validation_history:
  risk_level:

docs.title: Permission Model
docs.text:
Access follows minimum required permissions.

Request
  ↓
Permission Check
  ↓
Capability Validation
  ↓
Execution Approval
  ↓
Audit Event

docs.title: Worker Isolation
docs.text:
Workers operate in controlled environments.

Examples:

  • containers;
  • sandboxes;
  • restricted accounts;
  • temporary environments.

A worker receives only the access required for its task.

docs.title: MCP Security
docs.text:
External MCP connections require:

  • authentication;
  • authorization;
  • capability restrictions;
  • event logging;
  • revocation support.

docs.title: Capability Trust
docs.text:
New capabilities are not immediately trusted.

Lifecycle:

Unknown
 ↓
Tested
 ↓
Validated
 ↓
Trusted
 ↓
Monitored

docs.title: Application Security
docs.text:
Applications analyzed by AIOS must be treated as external entities.

AIOS tracks:

  • source;
  • versions;
  • permissions;
  • behavior;
  • test results;
  • security events.

docs.title: Evolution Safety
docs.text:
Self-improvement requires protection.

Every architectural change must have:

  • reason;
  • expected benefit;
  • risk assessment;
  • validation test;
  • rollback option.

docs.title: Audit System
docs.text:
Important actions create immutable events:

Action
 ↓
Security Event
 ↓
Audit Record
 ↓
Analysis

docs.title: Principle
docs.text:
AIOS must be powerful enough to evolve, but controlled enough to remain predictable.

Security is not a limitation of intelligence. It is the mechanism that allows intelligence to safely operate at scale.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Define the security architecture that protects AIOS identities, agents, nodes, memory, capabilities and operations.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Security in AIOS is based on identity, trust, permissions, isolation and verification.

An autonomous system must know not only what it can do, but also what it is allowed to do.

docs.title: Security Layers
docs.text:
Identity Layer
      ↓
Trust Layer
      ↓
Permission Layer
      ↓
Isolation Layer
      ↓
Audit Layer

docs.title: Identity Management
docs.text:
Every AIOS entity has a verifiable identity:

Identity:
  id:
  type:
  public_key:
  owner:
  created_at:
  status:

Entities include:

  • nodes;
  • agents;
  • workers;
  • capabilities;
  • services.

docs.title: Trust Model
docs.text:
Trust is dynamic and based on:

  • history;
  • successful operations;
  • validation results;
  • security events.
Trust:
  level:
  score:
  history:

docs.title: Permission System
docs.text:
Actions require explicit permissions:

Request
  ↓
Permission Check
  ↓
Policy Evaluation
  ↓
Allow / Deny

docs.title: Capability Security
docs.text:
Capabilities define their own boundaries:

CapabilityPolicy:
  allowed_actions:
  resources:
  restrictions:

docs.title: Worker Isolation
docs.text:
Workers execute inside controlled environments:

  • sandboxing;
  • resource limits;
  • restricted access;
  • monitoring.

docs.title: Memory Protection
docs.text:
AIOS memory requires:

  • access control;
  • provenance tracking;
  • integrity validation;
  • encrypted storage where required.

docs.title: Agent Security
docs.text:
Agents operate under:

  • identity verification;
  • role permissions;
  • action policies;
  • audit history.

docs.title: Audit System
docs.text:
All important actions create audit records:

AuditEvent:
  actor:
  action:
  target:
  timestamp:
  result:

docs.title: Security Events
docs.text:
Security incidents become AIOS events:

Detection
 ↓
Analysis
 ↓
Response
 ↓
Learning

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
After security problems:

Isolation
 ↓
Investigation
 ↓
Validation
 ↓
Restore Operation

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Security Framework provides the protection layer that allows autonomous intelligence to operate safely through identity, trust, permissions, isolation and continuous verification.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Define how AIOS stores, protects, synchronizes and restores its data, memory and knowledge across distributed environments.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Storage in AIOS is not only data persistence.

It is the foundation for continuity, memory preservation and distributed intelligence.

docs.title: Storage Layers
docs.text:
Application Data
       ↓
AIOS Object Storage
       ↓
Memory Storage
       ↓
Knowledge Storage
       ↓
Distributed Infrastructure

docs.title: Storage Objects
docs.text:
StorageObject:
  id:
  type:
  owner:
  version:
  location:
  integrity:
  metadata:

docs.title: Runtime Storage
docs.text:
Contains:

  • active processes;
  • temporary context;
  • execution state.

docs.title: Memory Storage
docs.text:
Contains:

  • short-term memory;
  • operational history;
  • agent experience.

docs.title: Knowledge Storage
docs.text:
Contains:

  • validated knowledge objects;
  • relationships;
  • evolution history.

docs.title: Archive Storage
docs.text:
Contains:

  • historical versions;
  • backups;
  • retired knowledge.

docs.title: Distributed Storage Model
docs.text:
AIOS supports multiple storage locations:

Node A
  |
  |
Node B ---- Distributed Memory
  |
Node C

docs.title: Replication
docs.text:
Important data may be replicated:

Primary Data
     ↓
Replication Policy
     ↓
Backup Nodes
     ↓
Validation

docs.title: Synchronization
docs.text:
Storage synchronization follows:

Change Detection
       ↓
Version Check
       ↓
Conflict Resolution
       ↓
Synchronization

docs.title: Data Integrity
docs.text:
AIOS validates:

  • checksums;
  • signatures;
  • versions;
  • provenance.

docs.title: Decentralized Memory
docs.text:
AIOS can support distributed memory approaches:

  • peer nodes;
  • content addressing;
  • replicated knowledge;
  • distributed archives.

docs.title: Backup and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery process:

Failure
 ↓
Detect
 ↓
Locate Backup
 ↓
Restore
 ↓
Validate

docs.title: Security Integration
docs.text:
Storage protection includes:

  • access control;
  • encryption where required;
  • audit records;
  • integrity verification.

docs.title: Evolution Integration
docs.text:
Storage history supports learning:

Stored Experience
       ↓
Analysis
       ↓
Knowledge Evolution
       ↓
Improved Storage Strategy

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Storage Architecture provides persistent, secure and distributed data foundations that allow intelligence, memory and knowledge to survive across nodes and over time.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Define how AIOS nodes exchange state, synchronize memory, share knowledge and maintain consistency across distributed environments.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text: Synchronization is the process that transforms independent nodes into a coordinated intelligence network.

docs.title: Sync Layers
docs.text:
Node Discovery
      ↓
Identity Verification
      ↓
State Exchange
      ↓
Conflict Resolution
      ↓
Synchronization
      ↓
Validation

docs.title: Node Discovery
docs.text:
AIOS nodes can discover peers through:

  • configured peers;
  • service discovery;
  • distributed network mechanisms.

docs.title: Node Identity
docs.text:
Every synchronization participant requires:

NodeIdentity:
  id:
  public_key:
  capabilities:
  trust_level:

docs.title: Synchronization Objects
docs.text:
SyncObject:
  id:
  type:
  version:
  timestamp:
  source_node:
  checksum:

docs.title: State Exchange
docs.text:
Nodes exchange:

  • memory updates;
  • knowledge objects;
  • configuration changes;
  • operational state.

docs.title: Version Management
docs.text:
AIOS tracks changes using:

  • versions;
  • timestamps;
  • provenance;
  • object history.

docs.title: Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
When nodes contain different versions:

Conflict Detection
        ↓
Comparison
        ↓
Trust Evaluation
        ↓
Resolution
        ↓
New Version

docs.title: Gossip Synchronization
docs.text:
Large networks can use gossip-style propagation:

Node A
 ↓
Peer Exchange
 ↓
Node B/C/D
 ↓
Network Convergence

docs.title: Distributed Memory Sync
docs.text:
Memory synchronization supports:

  • replicated knowledge;
  • shared experiences;
  • distributed learning.

docs.title: Offline Operation
docs.text:
Disconnected nodes can:

  • continue local operation;
  • store changes;
  • synchronize after reconnecting.

docs.title: Recovery After Network Split
docs.text:
Network Split
      ↓
Local Operation
      ↓
Reconnect
      ↓
Merge States
      ↓
Validate

docs.title: Security Integration
docs.text:
Synchronization requires:

  • authentication;
  • authorization;
  • integrity checks;
  • audit records.

docs.title: Evolution Integration
docs.text:
Sync history improves:

  • routing decisions;
  • replication policies;
  • network optimization.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Sync Architecture enables distributed nodes to operate as a coordinated intelligence system while preserving consistency, trust and knowledge continuity.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Define the complete lifecycle of task processing in AIOS from goal creation to execution, validation and learning.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
A task is the bridge between intention and action.

AIOS transforms goals into executable processes through planning, resource allocation and controlled execution.

docs.title: Task Object
docs.text:
Task:
  id:
  goal:
  priority:
  constraints:
  required_capabilities:
  assigned_agents:
  resources:
  status:
  result:

docs.title: Task Lifecycle
docs.text:
Goal Created
    ↓
Task Definition
    ↓
Planning
    ↓
Resource Allocation
    ↓
Agent Assignment
    ↓
Worker Execution
    ↓
Validation
    ↓
Learning Update

docs.title: Task Decomposition
docs.text:
Complex goals are divided into smaller tasks:

Large Goal
    ↓
Subtasks
    ↓
Dependencies
    ↓
Execution Plan

docs.title: Planning Integration
docs.text:
The Planner determines:

  • required capabilities;
  • execution strategy;
  • dependencies;
  • expected results.

docs.title: Agent Assignment
docs.text:
Tasks are assigned based on:

  • capability match;
  • trust level;
  • availability;
  • previous performance.

docs.title: Resource Integration
docs.text:
Before execution AIOS checks:

  • compute availability;
  • memory requirements;
  • storage needs;
  • network requirements.

docs.title: Execution Model
docs.text:
Task
 ↓
Agent
 ↓
Capability
 ↓
Worker
 ↓
Result

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Results are evaluated by:

  • correctness;
  • quality;
  • security compliance;
  • goal completion.

docs.title: Failure Handling
docs.text:
Failed tasks create recovery flows:

Failure
 ↓
Analysis
 ↓
Retry / Alternative Plan
 ↓
Execution
 ↓
Review

docs.title: Experience Collection
docs.text:
Every completed task creates experience data:

Execution
 ↓
Result
 ↓
Analysis
 ↓
Knowledge Update

docs.title: Evolution Integration
docs.text:
Task history improves future decisions:

Past Tasks
 ↓
Pattern Analysis
 ↓
Optimization
 ↓
Better Execution

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: AIOS Task Execution Model provides the operational lifecycle that connects goals, agents, resources and workers into a measurable, adaptive execution system.

docs.title: Связь с конституцией AIOS
docs.text: Этот модуль реализует статьи конституции AIOS (). Подробнее: , (). Без привязки к Octopus ().

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Define the execution layer of AIOS responsible for running capabilities through distributed workers.

Workers are replaceable execution resources. AIOS does not depend on a specific machine, container or environment.

docs.title: Worker Definition
docs.text:
A Worker is an execution entity capable of providing one or more capabilities.

Worker:
  id:
  type:
  environment:
  capabilities:
  resources:
  state:
  metrics:
  reputation:

docs.title: Worker Types
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Docker container;
  • Android emulator;
  • physical mobile device;
  • server process;
  • MCP module;
  • temporary external executor.

A five-minute external test worker is still a valuable AIOS resource if it produces useful knowledge.

docs.title: Worker Lifecycle
docs.text:
Discovered
    ↓
Registered
    ↓
Connected
    ↓
Available
    ↓
Executing
    ↓
Measured
    ↓
Reputation Updated
    ↓
Available

docs.title: Worker Scheduler
docs.text:
The scheduler selects workers using:

  • capability availability;
  • current workload;
  • performance history;
  • reliability;
  • resource cost;
  • location/environment requirements.

docs.title: Distributed Execution
docs.text:
AIOS can operate across multiple servers.

Example:

             AIOS Orchestrator
                    |
       +------------+------------+
       |            |            |
   Server A     Server B     Mobile Worker
       |            |            |
 Android Test  Web Test    Quick Benchmark

docs.title: Worker Cooperation
docs.text:
A worker may request assistance from another worker.

Example:

Worker A:
Needs public group data

Worker B:
Already has capability and free resources

Result:
Worker B provides data

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:
Every execution records:

  • execution time;
  • success/failure;
  • resource usage;
  • errors;
  • quality score;
  • environmental conditions.

docs.title: Temporary Workers
docs.text:
AIOS values opportunistic computation.

A worker can exist for a single purpose:

Create
 ↓
Execute test
 ↓
Collect experience
 ↓
Terminate

No permanent infrastructure is required for every capability.

docs.title: Worker Reputation
docs.text:
Reputation is calculated from historical execution results.

It affects future planning decisions.

docs.title: Security Principle
docs.text: Workers operate with minimum required permissions and isolated environments.

docs.title: Final Definition
docs.text: Workers are the distributed muscles of AIOS. They transform plans and capabilities into real-world execution while continuously generating experience for system improvement.
